title: heat wave to precede rain, snow and hailstorm in himachal, imd warns - The News Mill
search_term: heatwave (Bilaspur OR Chamba OR Hamirpur OR Kangra OR Kinnaur OR Kullu OR "Lahaul and Spiti" OR Mandi OR Shimla OR "Sirmaur (Sirmour)" OR Solan OR Una) Himachal Pradesh
full_text:
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a dual weather alert for Himachal Pradesh, forecasting severe heat wave conditions over the next two days, followed by widespread rain, snowfall, and hailstorms later this week due to a fresh Western Disturbance.
The latest bulletin from the Meteorological Centre in Shimla indicates dry weather is expected on May 26 and 27, leading to a sharp rise in temperatures across several districts. A “Yellow” alert for heat wave conditions has been issued for isolated areas of Una, Bilaspur, Hamirpur, Kangra, Solan, Shimla, and Sirmaur districts.
Officials anticipate both minimum and maximum temperatures to increase by 2 to 5 degrees Celsius in most parts of the state during this period. Una recorded the highest maximum temperature at 41.6 degrees Celsius in the past 24 hours, while Keylong recorded the lowest minimum temperature at 2.5 degrees Celsius.
The IMD forecasted a significant weather change starting May 28 as a fresh Western Disturbance affects northwest India. Under its influence, many places in Himachal Pradesh are expected to receive light to moderate rain and snowfall on May 28 and 29. Thunderstorms, lightning, hailstorms, and gusty winds of 40 to 50 kmph are predicted at several locations, with isolated areas possibly experiencing wind speeds up to 60 kmph.
High-altitude districts such as Kinnaur and Lahaul-Spiti may experience strong surface winds of 40 to 50 kmph during the disturbance, according to the IMD.
Following the wet spell, a sharp decline in temperatures is forecast, with maximum temperatures dropping by 2 to 5 degrees Celsius and minimum temperatures falling by 2 to 6 degrees Celsius over the next two to three days, providing relief from the prevailing heat.
The Met Office has issued advisories for residents and farmers in light of these changing weather conditions. People living in low hills and plain areas have been advised to avoid prolonged heat exposure, stay hydrated, and wear light cotton clothing. Farmers are urged to provide light and frequent irrigation to standing crops and adopt mulching to conserve soil moisture.
In the mid and higher hill regions, authorities warned that strong winds and hailstorms could damage crops, uproot temporary structures, and disrupt communication lines. Farmers have been advised to install hail nets to protect orchards and crops, while residents are urged to remain indoors during thunderstorms and to avoid shelter under trees or unstable structures.
The IMD stated weather conditions are likely to improve gradually from May 30 onwards, with only isolated light rain or snowfall expected in some parts of the state.
